lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Yield, cur!

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: Je pense que vous êtes le gentilhomme de bonne qualité.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Qualité calmie custore me! Art thou a gentleman? What is thy name? Discuss.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: O Seigneur Dieu!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: O, Signieur Dew should be a gentleman:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Perpend my words, O Signieur Dew, and mark;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: O Signieur Dew, thou diest on point of fox,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Except, O signieur, thou do give to me

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Egregious ransom.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: O, prenez miséricorde! ayez pitié de moi!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Moy shall not serve; I will have forty moys;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Or I will fetch thy rim out at thy throat

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: In drops of crimson blood.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: Est-il impossible d’échapper la force de ton bras?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Brass, cur!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Thou damned and luxurious mountain goat,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Offer’st me brass?

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: O pardonnez-moi!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Say’st thou me so? is that a ton of moys?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Come hither, boy: ask me this slave in French

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: What is his name.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Écoutez: comment êtes-vous appelé?

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: Monsieur le Fer.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: He says his name is Master Fer.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Master Fer! I’ll fer him, and firk him, and ferret him: discuss

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: the same in French unto him.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: I do not know the French for fer, and ferret, and firk.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Bid him prepare; for I will cut his throat.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: Que dit-il, monsieur?

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Il me commande de vous dire que vous faites vous prêt; car

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: ce soldat ici est disposé tout à cette heure de couper votre

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: gorge.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Owy, cuppele gorge, permafoy,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Peasant, unless thou give me crowns, brave crowns;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Or mangled shalt thou be by this my sword.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: O, je vous supplie, pour l’amour de Dieu, me pardonner! Je suis

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: le gentilhomme de bonne maison: gardez ma vie, et je vous donnerai

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: deux cents écus.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: What are his words?

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: He prays you to save his life: he is a gentleman of

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: a good house; and for his ransom he will give you two

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: hundred crowns.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Tell him my fury shall abate, and I

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The crowns will take.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: Petit monsieur, que dit-il?

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Encore qu’il est contre son jurement de pardonner aucun prisonnier, néanmoins, pour

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: les écus que vous l’avez promis, il est content de vous donner

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: la liberté, le franchisement.

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: Sur mes genoux je vous donne mille remercîments; et je m’estime heureux

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: que je suis tombé entre les mains d’un chevalier, je pense, le

lines.speaker: FRENCH SOLDIER
lines.text: plus brave, vaillant, et très distingué seigneur d’Angleterre.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Expound unto me, boy.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: He gives you, upon his knees, a thousand thanks; and he esteems

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: himself happy that he hath fallen into the hands of one, as

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: he thinks, the most brave, valorous, and thrice-worthy seigneur of England.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: As I suck blood, I will some mercy show. Follow me!

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Suivez-vous le grand capitaine. I did never know so full a voice

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: issue from so empty a heart: but the saying is true, “The

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: empty vessel makes the greatest sound.” Bardolph and Nym had ten times

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: more valour than this roaring devil i’ the old play, that everyone

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: may pare his nails with a wooden dagger; and they are both

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: hanged; and so would this be, if he durst steal anything adventurously.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: I must stay with the lackeys, with the luggage of our camp:

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: the French might have a good prey of us, if he knew

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: of it; for there is none to guard it but boys.

stage_directions.text: Alarum. Excursions. Enter Pistol, French Soldier, and Boy.

glosses.definition: mongrel dog — Pistol's insult to his prisoner

glosses.phrase: Je pense que vous êtes le gentilhomme de bonne qualité
glosses.definition: French: 'I think you are a gentleman of good quality' — the soldier's appeal to be spared
Pistol's garbled mishearing of the soldier's French — he has no idea what was said
French: 'O Lord God' — the soldier's exclamation
Pistol treats the French 'Seigneur Dieu' as the soldier's name — 'Mister Dew'
weigh carefully; consider — Pistol's affected Latinism
sword-point — 'fox' was slang for a sword, from a fox-head maker's mark
outstanding (large) ransom — 'egregious' retains its older sense of 'conspicuous'
French: 'take mercy' — 'have mercy'
French: 'have pity on me'
Pistol hears French 'moi' as a unit of measure — thinks the soldier is offering a small amount and demands forty
the lining of the belly; entrails

glosses.phrase: Est-il impossible d’échapper la force de ton bras
glosses.definition: French: 'Is it impossible to escape the force of your arm?'
Pistol mishears 'bras' (arm) as 'brass' (the metal) — thinks the soldier is insulting his honour by offering base metal
lecherous mountain goat — Pistol's absurd epithet
French: 'O pardon me'
a ton of (the things he calls 'moys') — Pistol's continued mis-hearing of 'moi'
French: 'Listen: what are you called?' — the Boy translating
French: 'Mr. the Iron' — his surname Le Fer means 'the iron'

glosses.phrase: fer him, and firk him, and ferret him
glosses.definition: Pistol's triple pun — 'fer' plays on the name, 'firk' = beat/trounce (also sexually), 'ferret' = harass
French: 'What does he say, sir?'

glosses.phrase: Il me commande de vous dire
glosses.definition: French: 'He orders me to tell you' — the Boy translates the threat back
French: 'make yourself ready'
French: 'cut your throat' — Pistol's famous 'couple a gorge' now in actual French
Pistol's attempt at French — 'oui, couper la gorge, par ma foi'

glosses.phrase: je vous supplie, pour l’amour de Dieu
glosses.definition: French: 'I beg you, for the love of God'
French: 'a gentleman of good family'
French: 'two hundred crowns' — the ransom offered
French: 'Little sir, what does he say?'

glosses.phrase: Encore qu’il est contre son jurement
glosses.definition: French: 'Although it is against his oath' — the Boy translates Pistol's grand face-saving reply
French: 'liberty, freedom' — the prisoner's release
French: 'a thousand thanks'
French: 'into the hands of a knight'

glosses.phrase: plus brave, vaillant, et très distingué seigneur d’Angleterre
glosses.definition: French: 'most brave, valorous, and most distinguished lord of England' — the Boy will translate this back literally
French: 'Follow the great captain' — the Boy telling the prisoner to go with Pistol

glosses.phrase: empty vessel makes the greatest sound
glosses.definition: proverb (from the Bible-era: 'empty vessels make most sound') — Pistol is noise without substance

glosses.phrase: roaring devil i’ the old play
glosses.definition: the devil from medieval mystery plays — a traditional noisy stock-villain who could be beaten with a wooden dagger by any comic

glosses.phrase: pare his nails with a wooden dagger
glosses.definition: the Vice-character's typical stage-business; a toothless threat

glosses.phrase: stay with the lackeys, with the luggage
glosses.definition: the boys guarded the baggage — a detail that becomes fatal in 4.7 when the French sack the camp and kill the boys

annotations.title: Bilingual comedy — the mishearing scene
annotations.body: The capture of Monsieur Le Fer by Pistol and the Boy is built on sustained linguistic misunderstanding. Pistol hears the French 'Seigneur Dieu' as the man's name; he hears 'moi' as a unit of currency ('forty moys'); he hears the soldier's 'bras' (arm) as English 'brass' (the metal) and takes offence at being offered base metal. The Boy, who actually knows French, works as translator between them. The scene is funny, but it is doing important structural work: it gives us our last sustained look at Pistol, the Boy, and the Eastcheap group before the French raid the baggage camp and kill the Boy offstage (reported in 4.7). The Boy's final soliloquy at lines 52–60 — that Pistol is a 'roaring devil in the old play' with more noise than valour, and that he (the Boy) must stay with the luggage because the French could raid the camp — is the audience's last notice of the Boy's situation. By the next scene-but-one, he will be dead. Shakespeare lets the Boy earn the last word, and then has him killed for being in its way.
sources: C. L. Barber, Shakespeare's Festive Comedy (1959)
Patricia Parker, Shakespeare from the Margins (1996)

trials.prompt: What drives the comedy of Pistol's interrogation of the French soldier?
options: A series of mishearings: 'Seigneur Dieu' taken as a name, 'moi' as a unit of currency, 'bras' (arm) as 'brass' (metal). The Boy mediates
A trick by the Constable of France

trials.prompt: The Boy's soliloquy at 4.4 lines 52–60 is the audience's last extended look at him. What does he reveal?
options: That he's plotting with the French
That he will stay with the luggage and lackeys (standard protocol for a boy non-combatant) because the French could easily raid the camp — foreshadowing his offstage death, reported in 4.7
That he wants to marry Alice
